figures.caption: Fig. 11–1.(a) An oxygen molecule with zero dipole moment. (b) The water molecule has a permanent dipole moment p0\Figp_0.

Fig. 11–2.(a) In a gas of polar molecules, the individual moments are oriented at random; the average moment in a small volume is zero. (b) When there is an electric field, there is some average alignment of the molecules.
Fig. 11–3.The energy of a dipole p0\Figp_0 in the field E\FigE is −p0⋅E-\Figp_0\cdot\FigE.
Fig. 11–4.Experimental measurements of the dielectric constant of water vapor at various temperatures.
Fig. 11–5.The field in a slot cut in a dielectric depends on the shape and orientation of the slot.
Fig. 11–6.The field at any point AA in a dielectric can be considered as the sum of the field in a spherical hole plus the field due to a spherical plug.
Fig. 11–7.The electric field of a uniformly polarized sphere.
Fig. 11–8.A complex crystal lattice can have a permanent intrinsic polarization P\FigP.
Fig. 11–9.The unit cell of BaTiO3_3. The atoms really fill up most of the space; for clarity, only the positions of their centers are shown.
Fig. 11–10.Models of a ferroelectric: (a) corresponds to an antiferroelectric, and (b) to a normal ferroelectric.
